Inspection and Repair 
@.1250 
Roof Hatches 
Inspect the condition of the roof hatches. Rectify any wear or damage found. 
Repair any chipped or damaged paint on the roof hatches. 
Inspect the condition of the roof hatch seals. Replace the seals if worn, damaged or torn. 
Check the roof hatch seals are correctly positioned on body side panel. Reposition the seal 
if necessary. 
After the roof hatches are installed, conduct a water test on the roof hatches. Ensure the seals 
prevent the ingress of water. Rectify any faults found. 
Roof Beam 
Check the condition of the roof bow. The bow must be smooth and without any damage. 
Inspect the roof beam for bend or twist. 
Inspect the roof beam for evidence of corrosion. 
Rectify any faults found, or replace the roof bow if necessary.

The locomotive is coated with a two-pack polyurethane paint over an epoxy primer. This com-
bination of surface finishes ensures full protection against corrosion. 
A top coat of Wattyl Poly-U 400 is applied over two coats of Wattyl Sigmacover CM primer 
to protect the steel against corrosion. The locomotive must be refinished to the same specifi-
cation to maintain corrosion resistance. 
Always observe the warnings when handling or applying surface finishes. 
WARNING: 
Uncured epoxy and polyurethane paints, and their solvents can be harmful if 
handled incorrectly Products of these types are FLAMMABLE. Wear protec-
tive clothing; including a particle respirator and impervious gloves. Always 
follow the precautions and directions of the manufacturer.

xterior Finish 
~reparation 
>repare the surface to be painted: 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
~epair any damage or corrosion found on the locomotive. If necessary, apply a corrosion inhi-
)itor to prevent the spread of corrosion. 
Plug all threaded holes and cover exposed threads to prevent the contamination with debris 
or paint. 
Remove any sharp edges, corners and burrs from the material. 
Remove any weld slag and spatter. 
If necessary, clean the surface using an abrasive blast cleaning technique as described in 
Section Sanitation Parts/Operating Material, then thoroughly clean the surface, remove de-
bris, grit and foreign material. 
Where the surface is not being blast cleaned, remove all loose or flaking paint. Roughen the 
existing paint using 400 grit emery paper. 
Mask any areas not being painted. 
Remove all foreign material, dirt, grease or other debris, then wipe the surface with solvent 
and a clean, lint-free cloth. Dry the surface with another clean, dry, lint free cloth. 
Remove all surface contaminates; grease, grime and oil processing soil using white spirit, 
trichloroethane or other suitable solvent. 
Priming 
Prime the surface with Wattyl Sigmacover CM primer, or equivalent. 
Mix, in a clean container, sufficient quantity of primer for the application. Mix the proportions 
according to the manufacturer's recommendations. 
Thin the mixture according to the application and manufacturer's recommendations. 
NOTE: 
Epoxy primers are not suited to application in temperatures below 4f> C. 
Apply the first coat of primer, approximately 250 microns thick when wet, and allow to dry ac-
cording to the manufacturer's directions. 
Apply a second coat of primer, approximately 250 microns thick when wet, and allow to dry.

Top Coat 
Coat the surface with Wattyl Poly U-400 paint, or equivalent. 
Exterior Finish 
Mix, in a clean container, sufficient quantity of paint for the application Mix the proportions ac-
cording to the manufacturer's recommendations. 
Stir thoroughly using a flat stirrer until a uniform consistency is achieved, then allow the mix-
ture to stand as specified by the manufacturer. 
Thin the mixture according to the application and the manufacturer's recommendations. 
NOTE: 
Premature setting may occur if freshly catalysed Polyurethane paint is added to 
paint that has been mixed for longer than 20 minutes. 
Apply a coat of finish, 65 microns thick (wet), to the entire surface, according to the manufac-
turer's recommendations. 
Allow the first coat to dry according to the manufacturer's specification, then apply a second 
coat in the same manner. 
Sanitation Parts/Operating Material 
@.1260 
Blast Cleaning 
Cover any area not being cleaned to prevent pitting the surface. 
To prevent damage to the threads and a build up of abrasive material, plug all threaded holes 
using suitable plastic plugs. 
Cover all exposed threads to prevent damage. 
Blast the surface clean to Class 21/2 standard, Near White Metal Blast Cleaning. Remove all 
mill scale, oxidation and foreign particles from the surface. The finished surface should be 
pitted to 125 microns deep, but need not have a consistent colour. 
Clean the surface, remove debris, grit and foreign material. 
Remove the plugs and other protective measures.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Draftgear and Couplers 
Also refer to BESCO's technical documentation.The draftgear and couplers are used to con-
nect the locomotive to the trainset. The draftgear is secured to the headstock on the under-
frame and houses the knuckle coupler. The draftgear is installed compressed into a pocket 
in the underframe. Once in position, retaining pins are removed and the draftgear expands 
into the pocket for a secure fit. The draftgear absorbs buffing and shunting forces during oper-
ation of the locomotive. 
The coupler pivots in the draftgear on a yoke pin which is held in position by a plate. A striker 
block prevents damage to the draftgear and locomotive by limiting the couplers range of 
movement. The striker block provides additional vertical support for the coupler. 
Rods and levers act as uncoupler handles to release the coupler. The handles are designed 
to allow release of the couplers from the side of the locomotive.

Miscellaneous Materials 
@.1200 
The procedures described in this chapter require multi-purpose grease. 
Before-Removal Operations 
@.1220 
Steam clean the draftgear, underframe in draftgear region, coupler, uncoupling handles, and 
surrounding area, remove all traces of dirt, debris and build-up of grease. Refer to Steam 
Cleaning in the Preface of this Volume. 
Damage to the components can result from improper application of the steam cleaning equip-
ment. Do not direct the spray stream into the axle box drain holes or into the traction motor 
ventilation openings.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Indian Raiftvays WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The locomotive is fitted with two rectangular windscreens in the end of each cab to provide 
protection for cab occupants from external conditions. Guard grilles are fitted ahead of the 
windscreens to prevent large objects from striking the windscreens. 
Side windows are located on each cab side wall ahead of the doorway to provide peripheral 
vision from the locomotive. Each window has a fixed triangular portion, and a sliding rectangu-
ar window which may be opened to provide additional ventilation. 
When closed, seals around the perimeter minimise the entry of water, dust and other debris. 
Excess water is caught by a gutter inside the cab, below the window. A channel ducts water 
rom the gutter to a drain hole in the forward hand rail alcove. A rubber flap seals the drain 
hole.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Horns and Lights 
(t'f 
The locomotive is fitted with headlights, marker lights, a strobe light and two warning horns. 
Dual sealed beam headlights are centrally fitted at each end of the locomotive. Both white and 
red marker lights are fitted to each side of each cab to provide clearance and directional in-
dication of the locomotive. 
An amber colour strobe light is fitted to the roof of each cab to act as a visual warning. 
Two pneumatically operated warning horns are fitted to the roof of each cab. Each horn has 
a different tone to suit the required operating conditions.

Before-Removal Operations 
@.1220 
Horns 
Open the cover marked "Wiper & Horn Isolation" on the driver's side of the cab console. Turn 
the horn isolation cock to the Off position. 
Strobe Light 
Set the "Lighting Front" circuit breaker (31 0.1) to the Off position. Circuit breaker 31 0.1/1 for 
the No. 1 End is situated in SB1 and circuit breaker 310.1/2 for the No.2 End is situated in 
SB2. For more information on circuit breakers, refer Chapter 8.2, MR Control. 
Headlights 
Set the "Lighting Front" circuit breaker (31 0.1) to the Off position. Circuit breaker 310.1/1 for 
the No. 1 End is situated in SB1 and circuit breaker 310.1/2 for the No. 2 End is situated in 
SB2. For more information on circuit breakers, refer Chapter 8.2, MR Control. 
Marker Lights 
Set the "Marker Lights" circuit breaker (31 0. 7) in cabinet SB2 to the Off position. For more 
information on circuit breakers, refer Chapter 8.2, MR Control.

Horns 
Indian Railways W AG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Inspect the condition of the horn gasket. Replace if worn, cracked or otherwise damaged. 
Additional information regarding the horns is provided in Chapter 2, Miscellaneous Compo-
nents of Volume F11, Suppliers Documentation. 
Strobe Light 
Inspect the condition of the strobe light cover. Replace if damaged or defective. Check the 
condition of the rubber gasket, replace if necessary. Clean serviceable covers using a clean-
ing solution suitable for acrylic materials. 
Inspect the condition of the strobe light gasket. Replace if worn, cracked or otherwise dam-
aged. 
Clean the strobe light lens using a cleaning solution suitable for use on acrylic materials. Re-
place cover if cracked, chipped or otherwise damaged. 
Additional information regarding the strobe light is provided in Chapter 6, Horns/Lights of Vol-
ume F1, Suppliers Documentation. 
Headlights 
Inspect the headlight lens for cracks or chips. Replace as necessary. 
Check the reflector for signs of oxidation, peeling or other damage. Replace sealed beam unit 
if necessary. 
Check the security of the headlight retaining ring. Tighten the knurled knob if necessary. 
Marker Lights 
Inspect the lenses for cracks or chips. Replace as necessary. Refer to Marker Light Lens in 
Section Removal. 
Check the security of the retaining ring. If necessary, loosen the setscrew, then tighten the 
knurled knob. Tighten the setscrew to secure the knurled knob against vibration.

After-Installations Operations 
@.1290 
Conduct a water test around the headlights, marker lights, strobe lights and warning horns 
and check for leakage, refer to Water Test in the Preface of this Volume. Rectify any faults 
found. 
Close the circuit breakers, then test the operation of the headlights, marker lights and strobe 
light. Rectify any faults found. 
Apply the pneumatic supply to the warning horns. 
WARNING: 
The warning horns are very loud, over 110 dB. Use hearing protection 
whenever testing, or sounding, the horns to prevent hearing damage. 
Move the locomotive to a suitable testing location. The horns are very loud. Wear hearing 
protection to avoid personal injury and ensure there are no bystanders within 50 meters. 
Test the operation of the horns and rectify any faults found.

Wiper/washer control 
Functional Description 
@.1140 
3 
/'}"--
Wipers and Washers 
3 
Washer pump 
The wipers and washers are used to enhance forward visibility in poor weather conditions by 
removing rain water and debris from the windscreens. There are two independent wiper motors, 
one for each windscreen, that operate the wiper arms. Water for the washer system is supplied 
from a single reservoir located on the assistant driver's side of the cabin. The wiper/washer sys-
tem is pneumatically operated using the locomotive air supply. In the event of a system failure, 
the wipers can be actuated by hand using the manual levers attached to the wiper motor shafts. 
Bombardier Transportation Ltd.

also required for the procedures described in this chapter: 
• A 17 mm diameter drift 
• A steel tube, with a 32 mm inside dimension 
• A blind M8 thread chaser 
• A honing stone

Miscellaneous Materials 
@.1200 
Miscellaneous materials required for the procedures described in this chapter include: 
• Loctite 262 
• Light machine oil 
• Suitable plastic plugs for sealing pipes and unions 
• Diluted alkaline solution

safety regulations. Any components washed in an alkaline solution must be 
thoroughly rinsed with clean, hot water.

To aid in reinstallation tag the location of the pipes connected to the wiper motor. 
Crack loose the pneumatic unions at the wiper motor. Allow any residual air to vent, then dis-
connect the pipes from the unions. Seal the pipes and unions on the motor to prevent the in-
gress of foreign material. 
Position a suitable receptacle below the wiper motor. 
Crack loose the water connections to the wiper motor. Allow any residual water to drain into 
the receptacle. Seal the pipes and unions to prevent further out-flow of liquid and the ingress 
of foreign material.

@.1250 
Wiper Arm 
Inspect the wiper arm for wear or damage. 
Wipers and Washers 
Check all the moving joints of the wiper and parallel arm for wear. Replace the arm assembly 
if the joints have excessive movement. 
Repair any chipped or worn paint as described in Chapter 1.2 Exterior Finish. 
Measure the distance between the centre of the clevis' at each end of the parallel arm. If 
necessary, adjust parallel arm length to the specification given in Section Technical Data. 
WIPER BlADE 
Inspect the condition of the windscreen wiper blades. Replace if worn or the rubber is 
deteriorated. 
WASHER NOZZLE 
WARNING: 
Alkaline solution is corrosive. Work according to the prevailing workshop 
safety regulations. 
Immerse the nozzle in an alkaline solution to remove any built-up deposits. Rinse thoroughly 
with clean, hot water, then dry nozzle using compressed air. 
NOTE: 
Do not enlarge the jet diameter. 
Check the jets for blockage. If necessary, clean the jet using a piece of thin wire. 
Test the nozzle and ensure the jets are clear.

Shocks generated during buffing and shunting are absorbed by the buffing elements. Once 
compressed, the buffer plunger rests on stops located on the buffer casing, transmitting 
shocks directly to the headstock. Recoil blocks absorb energy released as the buffer plunger 
returns to its unloaded position. 
An additional packing plate is added to the adjusting rod to obtain the correct installed length. 
Refer to Section Assembly. 
For further information on the buffers, refer to BESCO's Technical Documentation.

Bogies and Running Gear 
Functional Description 
@.1140 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The three axle, three motor Co-Co bogie assembly, is one of the major parts of the locomo-
tive. Two bogie assemblies support the entire weight of the locomotive and provide a means 
for transmission of the tractive effort to the rails. 
The bogies are designed to withstand the stresses and vibrations resulting from normal rolling 
stock applications. An important function of the bogie is to absorb and isolate shock caused 
by variations in the track. The suspension systems minimise the transmission of these shocks 
to the locomotive underframe. 
The traction motors are suspended in the bogie frame and on the individual axles. The motors 
transmit their torque to the driving axles through a gear mounted on the driving axle. The force 
from the driving axles is transmitted to the contact point between the wheel tread and the rail. 
Traction force is, in turn, transmitted through the axle journal boxes and guide rods to the bogie 
frame. 
The push-pull link rod, connected between the bogie transom and car underframe, transmits 
the tractive forces to the car body. 
Slowing or stopping the locomotive is achieved by use of electrically regenerative braking or 
pneumatically operated drum brakes. The brake cylinders and rigging are positioned to en-
able the brake shoes to clamp against the wheel tread surface. As with the tractive effort, brak-
ing effort is transmitted to the bogie frame by the axle journal boxes and guide rods and from 
the bogie frame to the locomotive by the traction rods. 
Isolation and absorption of shock loads and vibration is performed by the primary and second-
ary suspension. Movement between the car body and bogie is smoothly controlled by the pri-
nary and secondary suspension. Although the springs permit free movement in any direction, 
ateral buffers and dampers limit the amount and rate of lateral movement. Vertical dampers 
imit the amount and rate of the vertical bounce of the locomotive car body. Yaw (longitudinal) 
jampers control the car body pitch rate. Guide rods control the fore and aft movement be-
ween the axles and the bogie frame, while the link rod controls the fore and aft movement 
1etween the bogies and the locomotive car body. 
"he primary suspension, located between the axles and the bogie frame, is provided by twin 
oil springs on the axle journal box fore and aft of the axle line. Vertical hydraulic dampers 
n the end axles are used to dampen the rebound rate of the springs. This "Fiexicoil" arrange-
lent permits lateral movement of the axle. Longitudinal control of the axle, and the trans-
Jission of tractive and braking effort to the bogie frame, is provided by guide rods connected 
etween the axle journal boxes and bogie frame. Spheribloc rubber bushes in the guide rods 
!low the axle lateral movement without undue restriction.

Secondary suspension, is also provided by coil springs and vertical hydraulic dampers, lo-
cated between the bogie frame and the locomotive underframe on each side of the bogie. The 
weight of the locomotive car body is carried by the secondary suspension springs. The "Fiexi-
Fioat" arrangement of the secondary suspension allows the locomotive car body to move both 
laterally and vertically within certain limits relative to the bogies. 
The Co-Co bogie is fitted with wheel flange lubrication equipment to minimise wheel wear 
and sanding equipment is installed to provide additional traction between the wheels and rail 
when required.

Special Tools 
@.1190 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The bogies can be removed from the locomotive using either overhead lifting crane or jacks 
to raise the car body, or a drop table. Cranes or lifting equipment must have capacity to lift 
a total weight of 125 tons to allow bogie removal. 
An overhead crane or portable lifting device is required to remove and install some equipment 
on the bogie. 
IMPORTANT: 
Raise all four corners of the car body equally. Support the locomotive 
using safety stands. 
Miscellaneous Materials 
@.1200 
Plastic plugs, or alike suitable to seal pneumatic pipes. 
Plastic tape, or alike, 200 mm wide to seal the traction motor cooling inlet against the ingress 
of dirt, dust and foreign material.

@.1220 
Clean the traction motor bellows and the surrounding area. 
WARNING: 
Danger of High Voltage Electric Shock. To avoid personal injury, disconnect 
the loco from the overhead power supply. 
Use the Key Interlocking System to keep the electrical system earthed. 
High Pressure Pneumatic Systems. Ensure the bogie pneumatic system is de-
pressurised before commencing maintenance work.

Bogies and Running Gear 
Inspection and Repair 
@.1250 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Make a thorough inspection of the bogie and other bogie equipment. Repair or replace any 
damaged or worn parts. 
Conduct a thorough inspection to ensure all parts are in working condition. 
Clean the bogie frame, refer Section Bogie Frame, Sanitation Parts/Operating Material. 
Check the bogie frame thoroughly for wear, cracks or damage. Refer to Section Bogie Frame, 
Inspection and Repair. Inspect areas that may not normally be subjected to wear. Loose parts 
can cause damage to adjacent areas. 
Inspect all locating holes of rotating parts and pins, such as brake rigging and clevis pin holes, 
for cracks, wear or damage. 
Replace any bent or broken bolts, studs or pins. 
Check the condition of the bogie safety chain, pin and nut. Replace if worn or damaged. 
Clean all threaded holes and check the threads for wear that may result from loose compo-
nents. Remove the build-up of oil from the bottom of blind threaded holes. 
Inspect all rubber bushing for wear or damage. Press-out and replace bushes as required. 
Inspect bushing holes and the adjacent area for wear, damage or cracks. In most cases, dam-
age or out-of-round can be corrected by ring welding the hole, then reaming to the correct 
size. 
Check primary suspension coil springs, refer Section Wheelset. 
Check all hydraulic dampers, refer Section Primary Suspension and Secondary Suspension. 
Inspect axle bearing journals, bearings and axle boxes, refer Section Wheelset. 
Inspect link rod and vulkolland rings (link rod bushes), refer Section Traction Link. 
Inspect brake pads, rigging and piping, refer Section Air Supply & Pneu, Brakes. Rectify any 
faults found. 
Inspect the tread cleaning devices and check the thickness of the pad. Refer Section Brake 
l\ctuators. 
::;heck the wheel flange lubrication device, refer Chapter 2.08 Wheel Flange Lubrication.

Adjustments 
@.1310 
Wheel and Axle Load Adjustment 
IMPORTANT: 
Improper wheel or axle load adjustment will result in poor bogie 
performance. 
Record all adjustments in the bogie and locomotive logbooks for future 
reference. 
All springs vary slightly from batch to batch due to the manufacturing process. The springs 
used on WAG-9 locomotives are marked with one or two aluminium bands to identify their 
tolerance range. The number of bands also corresponds to the number of 2 mm thick com-
pensating plates below the spring. Before adjusting the wheel or axle loadings, check to en-
sure that all the primary and all the secondary suspension springs on the bogie are of the 
same tolerance range. Do not mix springs of different tolerance ranges. 
Adjustment of the wheel and axle loads is performed in the following sequence. 
Adjust individual wheel loads 
Adjust axle loads 
Check vertical bump stop clearance 
Wheel Load Adjustment 
Adjustment of individual wheel loads is performed by adding compensating plates between 
the axle boxes and primary suspension springs. The adjustment compensates for twist within 
the bogie frame or variations in spring lengths or stiffness. 
Wheel loads for a bogie are measured on a weighbridge. The bogie must be separated from 
the locomotive. A load equal to half the mass of the locomotive is applied to the bogie frame 
secondary suspension spring seats. The load is evenly distributed over the two seats.

Generally, compensating plates are added to the lightest wheel on the bogie. The load on the 
wheel were the compensating plate thickness is added will increase, while the load on the 
diagonally opposite wheel will reduce by the same amount. 
The compensating plates must be positioned between the spring and axle box. The thickness 
of compensating plates between each of the two springs on one axle box must be equal. An 
unequal thickness of compensating plates on the same axle box will result in poor bogie per-
formance. Removal of plates from the heaviest wheel is not recommend if it will reduce the 
number of 2 mm thick plates to less than the number of aluminium bands on the spring. A 
reduction in clearance between the axles and bogie frame may result. 
Wheel load adjustment is performed on an assembled bogie. The traction motors and link rod 
pivot head must be installed. A mass of 67 kg, half the weight of the link rod, must be added 
to the frame at the centre of the link rod end transom. 
Disconnect the primary suspension dampers from the bogie frame lugs. Place packing be-
tween the axle box and the damper to ensure the damper will not foul on the frame during the 
procedure. 
Remove the secondary suspension springs, lateral, vertical and yaw dampers from the bogie 
frame. 
Half the weight of the yaw dampers is applied to the bogie frame. Masses of 19.1 kg, half the 
weight of a yaw damper, must be added to each bogie frame yaw damper mounting point. 
NOTE: 
Masses of the other dampers and secondary springs are included in the applied 
body mass. 
Position the bogie assembly on a suitable weighbridge with each wheel centrally positioned 
over a load cell. 
Using hydraulic rams, apply a load of 130 ± 2 kN to each side of the bogie at the secondary 
suspension spring seats. DO NOT apply a combined load to the centre of the bogie frame. 
NOTE: 
The applied body load is equal to the maximum allowable locomotive weight for a 
nominal axle load of 20.5 tonnes. 
Measure and record the individual wheel loads. 
The difference between wheel loads on any axle, and the between axles must not exceed 
400 kg.

If necessary, adjust the wheel loads by adding compensating plates between the axle box and 
primary suspension spring. Use the following procedure to determine the number and thick-
ness of plates required. 
Place shim(s) between the lightest wheel and the rail until the wheel load is (are) within specifi-
cation. Compensating plate are available in 0.4, 1.0 and 2.0 mm thicknesses. Shims of the 
same thickness should be used. 
NOTE: 
One millimetre of packing increase the whee/load by 150 kg and will decrease the 
load on the diagonally opposite wheel by the same amount. 
Check the other wheels and adjust as necessary to obtain the specified wheel loads for all 
wheels on the bogie. 
Disconnect the guide rods at the axle box as described in Chapter 2.02 Section Disassembly. 
Disconnect the gear box support arm as described in Chapter 2.06 Section Disassembly. 
Disconnect the drive coupling between the traction motor and gear box as described in 
Chapter 2.06 Section Disassembly. 
Install the compensating plates between the axle boxes and primary suspension springs. 
NOTE: 
Compensating plates equal to the thickness of the shims are installed under each 
primary suspension spring of an axle box. DO NOT divide the shim thickness be-
tween the two springs. 
DO NOT place the compensating plates above the springs or weld the plates to the 
axle box or bogie frame.

Reassemble the bogie: 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Connect the guide rods at the axle box as described in Chapter 2.02 Section Installation. 
• Install the gear box support arm as described in Chapter 2.06 Section Installation. 
• Install the drive coupling between the traction motor and gear box as described in 
Section 0206.1280. 
Remove the shims between the wheels and rail, then recheck the axle loads. If necessary, 
readjust the thickness of the compensating plates to obtain axle loadings within specification. 
Measure the distance between the axle and bogie frame. The distance must be within the 
range of 27 - 35 mm. 
NOTE: 
If the clearance is less than 27 mm, all the primary suspension springs on the 
bogie must be packed with a thickness of compensating plates equal to the differ-
ence between the minimum specification and the actual clearance. 
Axle Load Adjustment 
Adjustment of individual axle loads is performed by adding compensating plates between the 
bogie frame and the secondary suspension springs. The adjustment compensates for vari-
ations in the bogie frame, locomotive underframe and spring lengths or stiffness. 
Wheel loads are measured on a weigh bridge with both bogies and the locomotive assembled. 
Generally, axle loads are adjusted by adding compensating plates under the two secondary 
suspension springs closest to the lightest axle on the bogie; either the two inboard or the two 
outboard springs. Adding compensating plates will increase the axle load on the closest axle 
by approximately 12 kg per 1 mm of plate thickness. The load on the other axle of the same 
bogie will reduce by the same amount. 
Wheel loads on one side of the bogie can be adjusted by adding compensating plates under 
the secondary springs on one side of the bogie only; either both right hand or both left hand 
side springs. Adding compensating plates below secondary suspension springs on one side 
of one bogie only will increase the axle load on the adjacent wheels by approximately 70 kg 
per 1 mm of plate thickness. The load on the diagonally opposite wheels of the other bogie 
will reduce by the same amount. Individual wheel loadings can only be adjusted by packing 
~he primary suspension springs as described in WHEEL LOAD ADJUSTMENT. 
rhe compensating plates are always positioned between the bogie frame and secondary sus-
>ension springs. Removal of plates from the heaviest axle is not recommended if it will reduce 
he number of 2 mm thick plates to less than the number of aluminium bands on the spring.

Axle load adjustment is performed with both fully assembled bogies attached to the locomo-
tive. The link rod must be installed between the bogie and locomotive underframe. 
Disconnect the secondary suspension dampers from the bogie frame. 
Position the locomotive on a suitable weighbridge with each wheel centrally positioned over 
a load cell. 
Measure and record the axle loads. 
NOTE: 
The nominal axle load is 20.5 tonnes. Axle loads must be within ± 2% of the nom-
inal axle load; 19.306-20.094 tonnes. 
Whee/loads must be within 4% of the measured axle load. 
Disconnect the safety chains between the bogies and locomotive underframe. 
If necessary, adjust the axle loadings by adding compensating plates between the bogie 
frame and secondary suspension springs. 
NOTE: 
Compensating plates are available in 2.0, 3.0 and 5.0 mm thicknesses. 
DO NOT place the compensating plates above the springs or weld the plates to the 
locomotive underframe or bogie frame. 
Recheck the axle loads and readjust if necessary to obtain the specified axle loadings. 
Measure the distance between the vertical bump stops as described in VERTICAL BUMP 
STOP CLEARANCE. 
Reconnect the secondary suspension dampers to the bogie frame. 
Reconnect the safety chains between the bogies and locomotive underframe.

Bogie Frame 
Functional Description 
@.1140 
Bogie Frame 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The bogie frame consists of two longitudinal sections, which form the side members, and 
three lateral transoms, which form the cross members. The cross members are welded to the 
side members forming a rectangular frame. 
The side and cross members are fabricated from 350L-15 high strength low-alloy steel. The 
link rod pivot assembly is welded to the centre of the end cross member. All structural welds 
on the bogie are ultrasonically tested during manufacture for penetration. 
The axle guide rod support brackets are of cast steel and are welded to the bottom flange of 
the side members. The axle spring guides and primary suspension spring pads are welded 
to the bottom flange of the side members as are the mounting brackets for the sanding equip-
ment and wheel flange lubrication. Secondary suspension spring guides, longitudinal (yaw) 
damper brackets and vertical bump stops are all welded to the top flange of the side members. 
Brackets for the vertical dampers and pivot brackets for the rebound limiting chains are 
welded to the outer webs of the side members. Brackets for pneumatic piping and wheel 
flange lubrication pipes are welded to the webs of the side and cross members. Lateral 
damper and bump stop brackets are welded to the top flange of the side members. Brackets 
for mounting the traction motors and transmission are welded to the cross members. Anchor-
age points for the brake rigging are located on the inside web of the cross members. 
To ensure full protection against corrosion all box sections are sealed against the entry of 
moisture and the frame is coated with a two-pack polyurethane paint over an epoxy primer.

described below. 
Non-Destructive Testing Equipment 
Non-destructive testing equipment, such as x-ray or ultrasonic, is required to inspect welds 
on the bogie frame. Operate the equipment in accordance with the manufacturer's directions. 
Additionally, special tools for examining welded areas are required.

Before-Removal Operations 
@.1220 
All parts and equipment should be removed from the bogie frame to facilitate cleaning and 
inspection. 
For immersion in alkaline solution, remove the pneumatic piping from the bogie frame. Mois-
ture in the pneumatic pipes can lead to corrosion and reduced brake performance. Otherwise, 
seal pneumatic pipes with suitable plastic plugs to prevent the ingress of dirt, moisture and 
foreign material. 
Remove the link rod pivot head, refer Section Traction Link, Removal. Cover the spigot to pre-
vent damage. 
Remove the lateral and vertical bumps stops from the bump stop webbing on the centre cross 
member. 
Remove all rubber hoses and other rubber components.

Inspection and Repair 
@.1250 
Inspection 
Bogie Frame 
t1~ 
Clean the bogie frame by immersion in a tank of alkaline solution. Refer Section Sanitation 
Parts/Operating Material. 
If necessary, blast clean the frame, remove all rust and build-up of debris, refer Section Sa-
nitation Parts/Operating Material. 
Clean all threaded holes and check the threads for wear that may result from loose compo-
nents. Remove the build-up of oil from the bottom of blind threaded holes. 
Welding on the bogie frame must be performed by a qualified welding technician. Welding in 
critical areas must be tested for penetration using a non-destructive technique. Do not weld 
in areas designated non-weldable. 
Any welding to the bogie frame is performed using Gas Metal Arc (GMA) welding equipment 
with constant-voltage characteristics and capacity suitable for welding plate up to 25 mm 
thick. The electrical connection is made with the electrode positive. ES4S copper coated 
welding wire is used with Argoshield 51 inert gas. 
Inspect the bogie frame for wear, fatigue, damage or cracking. In most cases, wear or damage 
in non-critical areas can be corrected by welding. In some instances it may be necessary to 
add reinforcement plates to the area, or drill relief holes to prevent the spread of cracks. Out-
of-round holes can be ring welded, then reaming to the correct size. 
Inspect all bushing holes and the adjacent area for wear, damage or cracks. 
Check the bogie frame thoroughly for wear, cracks or damage. Inspect areas that may not 
normally be subjected to wear. Loose parts can cause damage to adjacent areas. 
Carefully inspect the brake caliper mounting arms and the welds to the side member for wear, 
fatigue, cracks or damage. 
Check all the damper, traction motor, gear box and guide rod mounting lugs and the adjacent 
members for wear, fatigue, cracks or damage. 
Inspect the spring seat areas for signs of excessive wear. 
Inspect the lateral and vertical bumps stop webbing on the centre cross member for excessive 
wear or cracking. 
Inspect the safety pin eye at the side member for wear or cracks. Ring weld the hole, then 
ream to the correct diameter if required.

Bogie Frame 
Alkaline Solution Is Corrosive. Work According To The Prevailing Workshop 
Safety Regulations. 
NOTE: 
Remove all rubber parts from the bogie frame before immersion in alkaline solution. 
If necessary, loosen any built-up debris and foreign material using appropriate tools. 
NOTE: 
• Remove the pneumatic piping before immersing the bogie frame in alkaline solution. 
Immerse the bogie frame in a cleaning tank containing an alkaline solution. 
•After sufficient time to ensure removal of all foreign material, remove the frame from the tank 
and rinse thoroughly with hot water. 
Scrape any loose or flaking paint using a wire brush. 
If necessary, blast clean the bogie frame. Otherwise inspect the frame as described in Section 
Inspection and Repair. 
Refinish any exposed metal as described in Section Assembly. 
Blast Cleaning 
Cover the link rod pivot spigot to prevent abrasion of its surface. Blast cleaning will pit the sur-
face and result in reduced operating life of the Aclathan rings (link rod bushes). 
To prevent damage to the threads and a build up of abrasive material, plug all threaded holes 
using suitable plastic plugs. 
Cover all exposed threads to prevent damage. 
Blast the surface clean to Class 2 1/ 2 standard, Near White Metal Blast Cleaning. Remove 
all mill scale, oxidation and foreign particles from the surface. The finished surface should be 
pitted to 125 microns deep, but need not have a consistent colour. 
Clean the surface, remove debris, grit and foreign material. 
Remove the plugs and other protective measures.

Assembly 
0.1270 
Surface Finishes 
The bogie frame is coated with a two-pack polyurethane paint over an epoxy primer. This 
combination of surface finishes ensures full protection against corrosion. 
A top coat of Wattyl Poly-U 400 is applied over two coats of Wattyl Sigmacover CMprimer 
to protect the bogie frame against corrosion. The bogie frame must be refinished to the same 
specification to maintain corrosion resistance. 
Always observe the warnings when handling or applying surface finishes. 
WARNING: 
Uncured epoxy and polyurethane paints, and their solvents can be harmful if 
handled incorrecfly. Products of these types are flammable. Wear protective 
clothing; including a particle respirator and impervious gloves. Always follow 
the precautions and directions of the manufacturer. 
Preparation 
Prepare the surface to be painted: 
Repair any damage or corrosion to the bogie frame. If necessary, apply a corrosion inhibitor 
to prevent the spread of corrosion. 
Plug or cover all threaded holes and exposed threads to prevent the contamination with de-
bris or paint. 
Remove all sharp edges, corners and burrs from the material. 
Remove all weld slag and spatter. 
If necessary, blast the surface clean using abrasive blast cleaning technique as described in 
Section Sanitation Parts/Operating Material, then thoroughly clean the surface, nimove de-
bris, grit and foreign material. 
Where the surface is not being blast cleaned, remove all loose or flaking paint. Roughen se-
cure paint using 400 grit emery paper.

rv 
•lC 
Remove all foreign material, dirt, grease or other debris, then wipe the surface with solvent 
and a clean, lint-free cloth. Dry the surface with another clean, dry, lint free cloth. 
Remove all surface contaminates; grease, grime and oil processing soil using white spirit, 
trichloroethane or other suitable solvent. 
Priming 
Prime the surface with Wattyl Sigmacover CM primer, or equivalent. 
Mix, in a clean container, sufficient quantity of primer for the application Mix the proportions 
according to the manufacturer's recommendations. 
Thin the mixture according to the application and manufacturer's recommendations. 
NOTE: 
Epoxy primers are not suited to application in temperatures below 4Q C. 
Apply the first coat of primer, approximately 250 microns thick when wet, and allow to dry ac-
cording to the manufacturer's directions. 
Apply a second coat of primer, approximately 250 microns thick when wet, and allow to dry. 
Top Coat 
Coat the surface with Wattyl Poly U-400 paint, or equivalent. 
Mix, in a clean container, sufficient quantity of paint for the application Mix the proportions ac-
cording to the manufacturer's recommendations. 
Stir thoroughly using a flat stirrer until a uniform consistency is achieved, then allow the mix-
ture to stand as specified by the manufacturer. 
Thin the mixture according to the application and the manufacturer's recommendations. 
NOTE: 
Premature setting may occur if freshly catalysed Polyurethane paint is added to 
paint that has been mixed for longer than 20 minutes. 
Apply a coat of finish, 65 microns thick (wet), to the entire surface, according to the manufac-
turer's recommendations. 
Allow the first coat to dry according to the manufacturer's specification, then apply the second 
coat in the same manner.

General Notes on Hazards in the Work Area 
@.1040 
WARNING: 
Most components on the bogie are heavy. Use lifting equipment to raise or 
lower heavy parts and ensure they are supported by safety stands. 
Some procedures require components to be heated. Personal injury can re-
sult from improper handling. Always wear protective clothing whenever 
handling hot parts. 
The whee/sets are heavy and may roll once disconnected from the frame. 
Chock the wheels securely to prevent movement during assembly or disas-
sembly. 
The primary and secondary suspension springs are held in position by the 
weight of the locomotive and bogie frame, respectively. The springs are under 
tension and are heavy. Whenever removing the springs use care to ensure the 
springs do not jump from their seats and cause injury or damage.

Axle guide 
A Co-Co locomotive is fitted with two bogies each containing three wheel sets. Each wheelset 
comprises an axle shaft, a drive gear, two Monobloc wheels, two axle boxes and guide rods, 
and two tapered roller bearings. 
All information, procedures and specifications regarding the axle bearing are located in the 
SKF roller bearing manual, see Supplier's Documentation Volume F2. Bearings should be 
returned to the manufacturer for overhaul. 
Information relating to the drive gear is in Chapter Transmission. Details of the axle shaft drive 
gear journal are included in this Chapter.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Wheel set 
Wheel set 
~~ 
Wheelsets; axle and wheels are matched sets. Care should be taken to keep the set together. 
In the event one of the wheels requires replacement, the new wheel must be machined down 
within tolerance of the other wheel on the axle. 
Wheel 
One piece Monobloc wheels are machined from a single casting. Wheel hub, tread profile and 
brake mounting blocks are machined to precision tolerances. The axle bore surface is ground 
to a 6.3 micron finish. The wheels are manufactured and tested to comply with India Railways 
standard IRS R 34-81. After machining, each wheel is balanced to UIC 812-3 standards and 
the value of the residual imbalance is stamped on the wheel hub. 
A groove on the inner wheel face provides a reference line for the minimum tread diameter. 
The wheel tread, not the flange, may wear or be machined down to the level of this groove. 
The tread and wheel flange are heat treaded for improved service life. 
An oil gallery is located within the wheel hub for "floating off' the wheels from the axle using 
hydraulic pressure. The hole is sealed with a screw plug and copper washer to prevent the 
ingress of dirt during service.

Axle Box 
Wheel set 
~3 
The axle box is cast aluminium alloy and machined to precision tolerances. After machining, 
each axle box is x-ray tested for flaws. 
Six different types of front covers are fitted to the ~xle boxes depending on the application and 
location on the locomotive. Lugs, cast as part of the front cover, provide mounting points for 
the primary suspension vertical dampers. 
Guide Rod 
The guide rod, which is bolted to the axle box housing and to the bogie frame, provides a longi-
tudinal guide for the axle box housing. The guide rod is fitted with spherical rubber joints 
(Spheriblocs) at each end, which provide positive longitudinal guidance while allowing negli-
gible resistance to lateral movement. 
The guide rods contact the bottom of the axle box and provide positive lift for the wheelset 
whenever the bogie is raised. 
Each axle box has one guide rod. There are six guide rods on the Co-Co bogie.

Before-Removal Operations 
@.1220 
The axle bearings are precision parts that require careful handling. Some procedures in this 
Chapter will expose the wheel bearings. It is important to prevent the ingress of dirt, dust or 
other foreign materials to the bearings or lubricant. Contamination will severely reduce the 
service life of the bearings and can cause damage to the journals. Dusty or exposed condi-
tions are not suitable for handling bearings. All operations on the wheelsets, axle boxes and 
bearings must be carried-out indoors. Ensure the workshop area is clean. 
Steam clean the bogies before undertaking any procedure that will expose the bearings or 
journals. Particular attention must be paid to ensure the axle boxes are thoroughly clean to 
prevent particles of foreign material entering the bearings during disassembly. 
New bearings are coated in rust preventative and packaged in protective cardboard cylinders. 
Do not unpack bearings until immediately before installation. Always check the condition and 
measure the journals and bores before installing bearings. 
Removal 
@.1230 
Wheel set 
This procedure describes the removal of the wheelsets from a bogie detached from the loco-
motive. The wheelsets can, if required, be removed while a bogie is under the locomotive. 
Ensure the weight is supported on the locomotive jacking pads and safety stands are posi-
tioned under the bogie frame. 
Each bogie has three wheelset assemblies. This procedure describes removal of one 
wheelset. Repeat the steps for the other wheel sets.

Disassembly 
@.1240 
Wheel Dismounting 
Wheel set 
Q_J-l·· 
Dismounting of the wheel is achieved by "floating off' using hydraulic pressure. The pro-
cedure uses pressurised fluid to separate the interference fits between the cylindrical sur-
faces of the wheel hub and axle journal. 
Once the hydraulic pressure exceeds the pressure created by the interference fit an oil film 
builds up between the contact areas. The film reduces friction between the surface. The parts 
can easily be displaced without scoring the mating surfaces. 
The work area, joint surfaces and oil gallery must be completely clean; free of all swart, dirt 
and other contaminates. 
Dismounting of the drive gear is described in Chapter 2.06 Section Removal. 
NOTE: 
Do not remove the plug in the oil injection gallery until just before the wheel is 
ready to be removed. 
Remove the wheel bearing as described in the SKF roller bearing manual, see Volume F2. 
Remove the labyrinth ring. Refer Section Removal. 
Clean the wheelset thoroughly. The area around plug must be completely clean. Entry of 
foreign material to the gallery will damage the axle journal or wheel hub. 
Mount the axle set in a wheel press. 
Secure the oil injector device to the wheel press. 
Assemble the high pressure pipe, oil injector and pressure gauge to the injector attachment. 
Dip the injector nozzle into pure mineral oil, Shell Hypoid GX or equivalent. 
Fill the oil feeder with lubricant. Hold the nozzle upwards and depress the valve slightly to 
bleed the air while filling. 
Attach the feeder to the oil injector, then open the valve. 
Operate the pump handle until clean lubricant, free from air, is expelled from the high pressure 
pipe. Close the valve.

Inspection and Repair 
@.1250 
Wheelset 
@.1250A 
Profile Correction 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Wheel tread profiles can be corrected using either wheelset turning equipment or an under-
floor wheel turning machine. Tie down lugs are provided on the axle box for the purpose of 
securing the wheelset during machining. 
Best accuracy is obtained by using wheelset turning equipment; which requires removal of 
the wheelsets from the bogie frame. 
The locomotive, or bogie, is positioned above underfloor wheel turning equipment and the 
wheels are machined in situ. Flange irregularities and out-of-round are transferred to the 
new contour because the wheel is centred on the worn flange. To minimise flange out-of-
round the wheelset should be machined using a wheel set turning equipment after two correc-
tions using under floor equipment. 
There are two types of profile rectification; full and partial correction. Full correction removes 
all worn material to restore the original tread and flange profile. Partial correction is used to 
preserve the areas consolidated during operation, while minimising the removal of material. 
Partial correction is recommended for most re-profiling work. Full correction removes a large 
amount of material from the wheel, subsequently reducing the service life of the wheel. Full 
correction should only be carried out when absolutely necessary.

The Spheriblocs in the axle guide rods are critical to maintaining wheel alignment. 
No damage or wear is permissible on either the metal or rubber parts. Discard

Spheribloc Axial Wear Test 
Wheel set 
zA-c 
The test for axial wear in the Spheribloc can be carried out on the Spheribloc even if mounted 
in the guide rod. 
Before measuring the Spheribloc for axial wear, remove the guide rod from the bogie and 
store for 48 hours. 
Position the Spheribloc in a press and support the collar on a piece steel tube. Ensure there 
is sufficient space within the tube to allow clearance of the rubber part of the Spheribloc. The 
collar of the Spheribloc must be supported around its entire perimeter while conducting the 
test. 
Apply an axial load of 50 kg to the Spheribloc cross-pin, then position the plunger of a dial 
indicator on the shoulder of the cross-pin and zero the dial indicator. 
Increase the load on the cross-pin to 1500 kg, one time only, then decrease the load to 50 
kg and maintain for one minute. 
Check the reading on the dial indicator. If the reading is below 0.6 mm, turn over the Spheri-
bloc and repeat the procedure on the other side. If the reading still remains below 0.6 mm the 
Spheribloc is serviceable. If either reading was greater than 0.6 mm, replace the Spheribloc.

Wheel Mounting 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Either of two methods are recommended for the installation of Monobloc wheels to the axle 
shaft; shrink fitting or cold press-on. 
Although the wheels are cold pressed onto the axle in production, shrink fitting recommended 
for maintenance procedures. Shrink fitting involves no risk of damage to either the wheel hub 
or axle journal and provides the best fit between wheel and axle. The oil film builds up evenly 
when floating off shrunk-on wheels facilitating easy wheel dismounting. Shrink fitting is nor-
mally carried out without the aid of lubricants. 
Shrink Fit Method 
Measure and inspect the wheel hub and axle journal as described in Section Inspection and 
Repair. 
If the wheel was pressed off, it is necessary to ensure that it has an adequate interference 
fit to enable it to be cold-pressed onto the original axle shaft and achieve the minimum contact 
force. 
Before the wheel is placed in a furnace for heating, check the hub bore for damage. Blend 
out any small scratches, and ensure the oil injection gallery is clean. 
The furnace temperature and heating time must be modulated to ensure the wheel tempera-
ture never exceeds 250 oc. Heat indicating crayons can be used to show the wheel tempera-
ture while heating. Heat the wheel to 250 oc. for 120- 140 minutes. 
Remove the wheel from the heat and position it on the axle. Before the wheel cools and 
shrinks check, and adjust if necessary, the distance between the outer wheel flange and the 
axle hub flange. Ensure the wheel is square and seated correctly on the axle journal. Specifi-
cations are provided in Section Technical Data. 
Wait 48 hours after installation then conduct a Wheel Contact Test. Specifications are given 
in Section Technical Data.

Cold-Press Method 
Wheel set 
'],6..~ 
Cold press-an of wheels is not recommended in service, however can be carried out if the 
circumstances dictate. 
Check the contact surfaces for damage scratches or burrs. Small scratches can be blended 
out using emery cloth. Burrs can be removed with a honing stone. 
Carefully align the axle, wheel hub and wheel press using a sprit level and a hair protractor. 
The wheel must fit perfectly against the mandrel (yoke spar) of the press to ensure that an 
even pressure is applied around the wheel hub. 
Apply sufficient Molykote Paste 6 lubricant, or equivalent, to prevent scoring either the wheel 
hub or axle journal during installation. 
Set the press for a contact force of 779-1,026 kN. Ensure the pressure recorder is operating. 
Press the wheel onto the axle until the outer wheel flange is correct distance from the outer 
edge of the axle hub flange. The dimension is described in Section Technical Data. 
Inspect the plotted diagram. Remove the wheel using the oil injection method described in 
Chapter 2.02 then repeat the press-on procedure if; 
• the maximum permissible press-on force was exceeded 
• a score mark is indicated by the diagram curve 
• the wheel jumped during the installation procedure 
• If the minimum press-an force was not achieved, where the press-an force falls short 
of the minimum by less than 5%, conduct a contact test after 48 hours. The wheelset is 
serviceable if the results exceed the minimum contact force by 15%. 
Conduct a contact test 48 hours after successful installation of the wheel. Specifications are 
given in Section, Technical Data 
File the plot diagrams in the wheelset or locomotive logbook.

Labyrinth Ring 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The labyrinth ring is an interference fit on the axle shaft and must be heated to facilitate fit-
ment. Measure the inside diameter of the labyrinth ring and the outside diameter of the axle 
shaft journal. Ensure the dimensions are within the limits specified in Section Technical Data. 
Clean the journal and labyrinth ring using clean, dry, lint free cloth. 
Heat the ring to 130- 150 oc. using a heating cabinet, induction heating equipment, a hot oil 
bath, or similar. 
WARNING: 
The labyrinth ring is heated to 130 - 150 o C. Personal injury can result from 
improper handling. Wear protective clothing whenever handling hot parts. 
NOTE: 
The installation operation must be performed rapidly. The ring will not fit if it be-
comes cool. 
Once the ring is at the specified temperature, remove it from the heat. Carefully clean the laby-
rinth ring and axle journal using a clean, dry lint free cloth. 
Install the ring on to the axle shaft with a slight twisting motion until the ring is squarely posi-
tioned against the shoulder.

Whenever new bearings or drive gears are installed the assembly should be tested actively 
before installation to a locomotive. 
Using a suitable jig, run the wheels at approximately 90 km/h for 20 minutes in each direction. 
The direction of rotation should be changed periodically during the test. 
CAUTION: 
During this test the axles and wheels are rotating. Use extreme care to en-
sure that no part of clothing, rags or hair will be caught in the rotating equip-
ment. 
Check the bearings for noise or overheat conditions that may indicate faulty parts or improper 
installation. 
Listen to the gear box for unusual sounds that indicates incorrect meshing of the drive gears.

Notes on Bearings and Lubrication 
Inspect and maintain the bearings. 
[Details to follow] 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Always clean around the grease nipple screw plug before removal. Discard the copper 
gasket. 
The bearings must be dismounted and cleaned according to the directions. 
[Details to follow] 
Never mix different grease types in the bearings. 
Always use a metering device whenever adding lubricant. Over lubricating will cause damage 
to the bearing as quickly as under lubrication does. 
In normal use the grease will become softer and darker with usage. Particles of steel from 
deteriorating bearings causes the grease to become a grey colour. 
Grease Quick Test 
Place a small amount of grease between two glass plates. Compress the plates together 
using finger pressure. Slide the plates back and forth. Contaminated grease will cause the 
glass plates to slide roughly. Clean, uncontaminated grease will allow the plates to slide 
smoothly over one another.

_Primary Suspension 
?-
Functional Description 
@.1140 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Isolation and absorption of shock loads and vibration between the bogie frame and wheelsets 
is performed by the primary suspension. The primary suspension, located between the axles 
and the bogie frame, comprises coil springs on the axle box and vertical hydraulic dampers. 
The middle axle has a safety link between the axle box and underframe. The link lifts the axle 
with the bogie frame when bogie is raised by lifting equipment. 
The vertical dampers are mounted on the end axles between the axle box front cover and lugs 
on the bogie frame and are used to dampen the rebound rate of the springs. End axles have 
a single coil spring situated on the axle box fore and aft of the axle centre line. Each end axle 
(wheelset) has four coil springs. The middle axle has an inner and an outer coil spring situated 
on the axle box fore and aft of the axle centre line. Each middle axle (wheelset) has eight coil 
springs. 
The springs are situated in seats of each axle box at each end of the wheel set. An insulating 
base and compensating plates are installed between each spring and the axle box to estab-
lish the correct wheel load rate. 
~ongitudinal control of the axle, and the transmission of tractive and braking effort to the bogie 
=rame, is provided by guide rods connected between the axle boxes and bogie frame. The 
'Fiexicoil" arrangement of the primary suspension permits lateral movement of the axle. 
3pheribloc rubber bushes in the guide rods allow the axle lateral movement without undue 
·astriction.

All springs vary slightly due to the manufac-
turing process. In smaller springs this differ-
ence is generally negligible, but on larger 
springs, such as used on the WAG-9 pri-
mary suspension, small variations can have 
a marked impact on performance. WAG-9 
locomotive springs are manufactured to ex-
acting standards, but variations between dif-
ferent batches do occur. Springs are marked 
with one or two aluminium bands (1) during 
manufacture to identify the spring's toler-
ance range. All springs on the bogie must be 
within the same tolerance range. Do not mix 
springs with different numbers of aluminium 
bands on a bogie.

were tagged to identify their location, rein-
stall the suspension components into the 
same position from which they were re-
moved. Whenever replacing a spring, al-
ways use a new spring with the same toler-
ance range as the other springs on the 
bogie.

Compensation Plates 
NOTE: 
Primary Suspension 
-:~1./ 
v·, 
Never install shims above the springs or weld the shims to the axle box. 
The variations that occur in springs, described in PRIMARY SUSPENSION SPRINGS, and 
softening over time is offset by compensation plates (shims). The shims are always posi-
tioned between the spring and the axle box. Springs are marked in production with one or two 
aluminium bands to indicate their tolerance range. Whenever installing new springs, place 
the number of 2 mm shims below the spring corresponding to the number of aluminium bands. 
Both springs in a set on an axle box must be packed with an equal thickness of compensation 
plates. Shims are available in 2 and 3 mm thicknesses. 
Adjustment of the axle loads is described in Chapter 2.00 Section Adjustments.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Secondary Suspension 
'f~ 
The primary and secondary suspension absorbs and isolates shock loads, due to track defor-
mities and other variations. The primary suspension is described in Section 2.03. 
The secondary suspension consists of coil springs and vertical, lateral and yaw dampers, lo-
cated between the bogie frame and the locomotive underframe on each side of the bogie. The 
springs support the weight of the locomotive car body and allow free movement between the 
car body and the bogie. This "FiexiFioat" arrangement of the secondary suspension allows 
the locomotive car body to move both laterally and vertically within certain limits relative to 
the bogies. 
Although the springs allow movement in any direction, lateral buffers and dampers limit the 
amount and rate of lateral movement, while rebound limit chains and vertical dampers limit 
the amount and rate of the vertical rebound of the locomotive car body. Longitudinal (yaw) 
dampers are provided to dampen the rate of pitch of the car body. Traction rods, connected 
between the bogie and locomotive underframe, control the fore and aft movement between 
the bogies and the locomotive. 
Insulating bases and compensating plates (shims) are installed between each spring and the 
bogie frame. Shims may be added to establish the correct wheel load levels. 
Bombardier Transportation Ltd.

Traction Link 
Functional Description 
@1140 
Indian Railways W AG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The traction link transmits traction and braking forces from the bogie to the locomotive super-
structure (underframe). Although the traction link maintains the relative longitudinal position 
ofthe bogie to the locomotive underframe, it permits lateral movement between the two struc-
tures. The link rod is situated between two pivot points, one on the locomotive underframe, 
the other on the end transom of the bogie, permitting lateral movement but restraining longi-
tudinal movement. Under normal conditions the rod is inclined at a 1 :1 0 slope towards the 
centre of the locomotive underframe. 
A pivot head, situated at each end of the link rod, has a ring of pliable material between the 
pivot post and head. The rings are secured to the pivot head by an outer retaining ring and 
a retaining plate bolted to the post. 
The rod is symmetrical end to end and the pivot heads are identical and interchangeable be-
tween the bogie and underframe. The link rod is secured to the locomotive underframe by 
safety cables.

In addition to conventional railways workshop tools and equipment, the following tools are 
also required for the procedures described in this chapter: 
• Fixed and portable presses for the removal and installation of some components. 
• A special tool to install the pivot heads to the locomotive underframe or bogie. The tool-
ing must be fabricated to ABB drawing V01875118. 
• M 12 thread chaser 
Miscellaneous Materials 
@1200 
Miscellaneous materials required for the procedures described in this chapter include: 
• ALTEMP Q NB50 paste 
• Glycerin, as lubricant. 
Before Removal Operations 
@1220 
Steam clean the link rod and pivot heads and surrounding area. Remove all traces of dirt, de-
bris and build-up of grease. Refer to Steam Cleaning in the Preface of Volume D1, Mainten-
ance and Repair. Damage to the components can result from improper application of the 
steam cleaning equipment. 
Do not direct the spray stream into the axle box drain holes or into the traction motor ventila-
tion openings.

Dismounting of the labyrinth ring (2) is 
achieved by "floating off' using hydraulic 
pressure and a suitable bearing puller tool. 
The procedure uses pressurised fluid to 
separate the interference fits between the 
cylindrical surfaces of the labyrinth ring (2) 
and axle journal. An oil gallery is located 
within labyrinth ring (2) for "floating off' the 
ring from the axle using hydraulic pressure. 
Once the hydraulic pressure exceeds the 
pressure created by the interference fit, an 
oil film builds up between the contact areas. 
The film reduces friction between the sur-
face. The parts can easily be displaced with-
out scoring the mating surfaces. 
The gallery is sealed with a screw plug (1) to 
prevent the ingress of foreign material. 
NOTE: 
Do not remove the oil gallery plug 
(1) until just prior to removing the 
labyrinth ring (2). 
Assemble the high pressure pipe, oil injector 
and pressure gauge to the injector attach-
ment. Dip the injector nozzle into pure min-
eral oil, Shell Hypoid GX or equivalent. 
Fill the oil feeder with oil. Hold the nozzle up-
wards and depress the valve slightly to 
bleed the air while filling. 
Attach the feeder to the oil injector, then 
open the valve. 
Operate the pump handle until clean oil, free 
from air, is expelled from the high pressure 
pipe. Close the valve. 
Bombardier Transportation Ltd.

Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
NOTE: 
Transmission -·~. v
fl.:~,., 
NOTE: 
The work area, joint surfaces and 
oil gallery must be completely 
clean; free of all swart, dirt and 
other contaminates. Entry of 
foreign material to the oil gallery 
will damage the axle journal or 
labyrinth ring (2). 
Clean around the plug (1) using com-
pressed air. Ensure that no dirt or debris is 
located near, or around the plug. When the 
area around the plug is completely clean, re-
move the plug (1) from the labyrinth ring (2). 
Connect the high pressure hose (3) to the 
labyrinth ring (2) using suitable adaptors. 
Slowly pump the injector (4) until oil exudes 
at the joint between the labyrinth ring (2) and 
axle shaft (1 ). The oil needs some time to fill 
the gallery and seat. Trying to pull off the 
labyrinth ring (2) too soon will damage the 
labyrinth ring (2) or axle journal. 
Slowly pull the labyrinth ring (2) from the axle 
using a suitable bearing puller. Sufficient oil 
is trapped within the seat for removal. Keep 
the pressure constant until the ring is re-
moved. 
Do not interrupt the pressing-off of the labyrinth ring. Stopping or pausing will 
cause the oil film to leak away from the contact surfaces. 
Bombardier Transportation Ltd.

@.1250 
Suspension Tube 
C206035

Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
Clean the drive assembly components. Remove all traces of dirt and debris, then pack the 
parts in a suitable rust preventative to minimise oxidation. Cover components to prevent dam-
age and contamination with foreign material, and store on wood or cardboard. 
Inspect the gear cases and suspension tube for damage. Test the gear case and suspension 
tube for cracks using ultrasonic, x-ray or other non-destructive testing techniques. If necess-
ary small cracks and minor damage can be repaired by welding. The repair procedure is de-
scribed in the Gear Box Casting and Weld Repair Procedure, Volume TBA. 
Measure the labyrinth ring and bearing seats in the suspension tube. Specifications are given 
in Section Technical Data. Recondition the suspension tube if out of specification. 
Check the mating surfaces of the suspension tube, and gear case. Surfaces must be in per-
fect condition to seal correctly. Small imperfections and burrs can be removed using fine 
emery paper. 
Check the labyrinth rings (1 ), (2), (4), (5), (6), (7), (8), (9), and (10) for wear or damage. Dimen-
sions are given in Section Technical Data. Replace any out of specification labyrinth rings. 
Check the supporting ring (3) for wear or damage. Replace the ring (3) if out of specification.

Bearings are precision parts and critical to the longevity of the drive assembly. 
Never use damaged, dirty or out of specification bearings. 
Clean the bearings, removing all traces of oil, grease and other foreign material. Inspect the 
bearings (7, 8). Replace the entire bearing if worn, damaged or pitted. The bearings must be 
in perfect condition if they are to be reinstalled. 
Check all the bolts for wear or damage. Replace the bolts if the shank is worn or damaged. 
Inspect the condition of the threads. If necessary, repair the thread using an appropriate 
thread chaser, or replace the pivot bolt. 
Do not reuse worn or damaged locking nuts. Discard locking nuts after 5 uses.

Release of the sand is controlled by the locomotive traction control system incorporated into 
the E70 brake unit. Sand is released from pneumatically controlled valves at the sand boxes 
and is exhausted through nozzles just ahead of wheels. 
Each bogie has four sand boxes adjacent to the end axle wheels.

Wheel Flange Lubrication 
Functional Description 
@.1140 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The flange lubrication is pneumatically operated by control valves located on the Brake 
Frame, which are controlled by the Memotel system. Lubricant is periodically released from 
the reservoir tank through a pneumatically operated distribution valve and is sprayed onto the 
wheel flanges from nozzles situated above each end wheel. 
A pneumatic and hydraulic pipe is connected to each nozzle. The hydraulic piping connects 
the distribution valve to the nozzles on the individual wheels. The pneumatic piping provides 
pressured air to create a low pressure point at each nozzle. The low pressure point at the tip 
of the nozzle draws oil through the hydraulic piping and sprays atomised oil onto the wheel 
flanges.

Two types of piping are associated with the 
flange lubrication system. The pneumatic 
system (2) provides pressured air to operate 
the distribution valve and to atomise the lu-
bricant at the nozzle (5). The hydraulic sys-
tem (1) distributes the lubricant from the res-
ervoir to the nozzles (5) on the individual 
wheels. 
Pneumatic Piping 
The pneumatic piping (3) can be removed 
as required using normal workshop prac-
tices. 
Close the air supply to the bogie. 
Crack loose the pneumatic connection (7) at 
the union. Allow any residual pressure to 
vent, then disconnect the pneumatic piping 
(3) from the union. 
Seal the pneumatic piping (3) using suitable 
plugs to prevent the ingress of foreign ma-
terial. 
Hydraulic Piping 
The hydraulic piping (4) can be removed as 
required using normal workshop practices. 
Place a suitable receptacle below the area. 
Crack loose the hydraulic connection (6) at the 
union. Allow any residual lubricant to drain, 
then disconnect the pipe (5) from the union. 
Seal the pipe (4) and union using suitable 
plugs to prevent the ingress of foreign ma-
terial.

Roof Line 
Numbers in brackets () denote the electrical equipment apparatus item number used in the 
locomotive schematics, refer to Volume G, Cabling Documentation. 
Functional Description 
@,1140 
The ronf line carries the current from the pantograph via the isolator switches. The line is elec-
trically insulated from the roof by the pin insulators. Current from the roof line passes through 
the vacuum circuit breaker and high voltage connection to the high voltage bushing on the 
roof. Current is carried to the transformer, situated in the machine room, along the high volt-
age cable. The primary current transformer is situated on the underside of the converter roof 
hatch, below the high voltage bushing. The control electronics uses the signal to measure the 
current flowing through the high voltage cable from the catenary. 
The isolator switches allow the roof line to be broken for removal of the locomotive roof 
hatches. Spring contacts hold the isolator switches in either position. 
The high voltage cable connects to the high voltage bushing with a push on-pull off connec-
tion. The cable is secured to the converter roof hatch by a clamp and to the machine room 
along a support arm.

Miscellaneous Materials 
@.1200 
_;St 
Roof Line 
Miscellaneous materials required for the procedures described in this chapter include: 
• Rhodorsil Paste 4 silicon grease is required as a moisture-proofing agent for the roof 
bushing and pin insulators 
• Clean plastic is required as a covering to protect the high voltage bushing and cable 
electrical contact surfaces 
• Tape to secure the plastic 
• Secheron Grease L3, NBT 400344 P2, is required to coat the earthing switch contact 
blades and spring contacts 
• Silicon spray to lubricate the high voltage cable connections 
• Clean, dry lint free cloth 
• Paper towelling 
Before Removal Operations 
@.1220 
Lower the pantograph and isolate the overhead catenary. Earth the locomotive at the vacuum 
circuit breaker. Refer to the Preface of this Volume. 
Earth the traction converter as described in Chapter 4.1, Traction Converter. Operation of the 
key interlocking system is described in Chapter 3.11, Protection Concept of Volume A, 
Driver's Manual. 
Steam clean the roof line and surrounding area, paying particular attention to the contact 
springs. Remove all traces of dirt, debris and build-up of grease. Refer to Steam Cleaning 
in the Preface of this Volume.

Inspection and Repair 
@.1250 
High voltage cable 
'S''l. \' 
Roof Line 
Check for external damage to the cable case and the connectors. Replace the cable if dam-
aged. 
Check the electrical connectors. The contact surfaces must be in good condition and com-
pletely clean. 
High voltage bushing 
Check the condition of the bushing and replace if damaged or cracked. 
Inspect the electrical connector. Replace the bushing if worn, damaged or if there is any sign 
of arcing or carbon build-up. 
Clean the electrical connectors. The contact surfaces must be in good condition and com-
pletely clean. 
Inspect the condition of the silicon coating. Contaminants in the silicon coating reduce the 
insulating effect ofthe high voltage bushing insulator. Water applied to the surface of the insu-
lator should bead and not wet the surface. If the coating is contaminated or is wetted by water, 
the coating must be renewed. Audible discharges or flashovers that are visible in the dark are 
an indication that the insulator needs to be re-coated. 
Remove the contaminated silicon coating using paper towelling. Wipe any remaining silicon 
coating from the insulator using a clean, dry, lint free cloth. Clean the surface of the insulator 
thoroughly using a cloth soaked in alcohol. 
Ensure the glazed surface of the high voltage bushing insulator is clean, dry and shiny. The 
silicon grease described in Section Miscellaneous Materials should be applied evenly to the 
entire glazed surface of the insulator to a thickness of 0.2 to 1 mm. 
Take care when handling the high voltage bushing after coating to avoid contamination of the 
silicon grease on the insulator.

Inspect the condition of the roof line and clamps. Replace any bent or damaged parts. 
Inspect the condition of the jumper cables for damage, evidence of arcing or fraying. Replace 
the cables if damaged or defective. 
Isolator switches 
Inspect the condition of the contact springs. Replace the contact springs if worn, cracked, 
damaged or bent. 
Check the condition of the spacers. Replace if damaged. 
Inspect the electrical connection areas. Replace the arm or contact springs if worn, damaged 
or if there is any sign of arching or carbon build-up. 
Clean the electrical connections. The contact surfaces must be in good condition and com-
pletely clean. 
Pin insulators 
Inspect the condition of the pin insulators, replace if cracked, chipped or damaged. 
Inspect the condition of the silicon coating. Contaminants in the silicon coating reduce the 
insulating effect ofthe insulator. Water applied to the surface ofthe insulator should bead and 
not wet the surface. If the coating is contaminated or is wetted by water, the coating must be 
renewed. Audible discharges or flashovers that are visible in the dark are an indication that 
the insulator needs to be re-coated. 
Remove the contaminated silicon coating using paper towelling. Wipe any remaining silicon 
coating from the insulator using a clean, dry, lint free cloth. Clean the surface of the insulator 
thoroughly using a cloth soaked in alcohol. 
Ensure the glazed surface of the insulator is clean, dry and shiny. The silicon grease de-
scribed in Section Miscellaneous Materials should be applied evenly to the entire glazed sur-
face of the insulator to a thickness of 0.2 to 1 mm. 
Take care when handling the insulator after coating to avoid contamination of the silicon 
grease.

Functional Description 
@.1140 
Main features and purpose 
Indian Railways WAG-9 
Maintenance and Repair Manual 
The single-arm pantograph type ESiD 1 03-2500 was primarily adapted to suit the needs of 
Indian Railways. The well proved single-arm pantograph, type ES, was used as the basic 
model. The brief was to realise a compactly constructed pantograph with an installation height 
that was as low as possible. The solution was found with the help of a new technical driving 
system in, which the tension springs are stretched by an pneumatic drive, then fully released 
again in a lowered position by the same means. The following characteristics were achieved 
by means of these and other new adaptions: 
Compact, robust construction 
Installation height of 330 mm without insulator 
Simple air cylinder with no compression spring system 
No expensive pneumatic control system 
Good dynamic properties independent of the direction of travel 
No lifting of the lowered pantograph at high speeds 
Constant contact pressure on the separately and individually suspended sliding strips 
Predetermined breaking-point by means of a mechanical shearing pin as a fast-operating 
safeguard against catenary damage 
Automatic lowering device at a lifting height of 2000 mm 
Increased operating safety through use of stainless steel in essential parts of the pantograph 
Construction 
Base frame 
The base frame is carried by 4 support insulators. It consists of a welded assembly with rec-
tangular profiled steel pipes. The bearings for the main shaft and the auxiliary shaft, the arms 
for the support insulators and the air connector are located on the two longitudinal supports. 
The damper fixings, the current connectors, the bearings for the push rod and the supports 
for the air cylinder and the safety valves are located on crossbeams. 
Chapter 3.02 - Page 2 I 16

Indian Railways WAG-9

Lower and upper arms 
Pantograp~ \Y 
The lower arm consists of a steel pipe assembly with the main shaft system held by the shear-
ing pin. The bearings to receive the upper arms are attached to the top end, and the bearings 
for installing the damper to the bottom part. 
The upper arms are made of graduated aluminium tubing; the bosses to receive the lower 
arm and the bearing bushes for receiving the collector head tube are welded on to the ends 
of these. A bearing plate for mounting the support bracket linking the upper arms is fixed to 
the lower part of each upper arm. This support bracket also serves as the top bearing for the 
push rod and to receive the upper shearing pin. 
Push rod 
The push rod consists of a steel tube with a bearing bush welded to the upper end and a 
screwed bolt welded into the lower end to adjust its length. 
Collector head tube and collector head 
The collector head tube constructed of aluminium, links the two upper arms of the collector 
head and serves to receive the straight-line guide rod. 
The collector head consists of two rustproof collector head bearers with two vertical sliding-
strip spring actions attached to either end of both. These support two sliding strip bearers and 
these in turn each support a sliding strip made of unhardened, silver-coated spring steel. The 
two sliding strip are suspended independently of one another, providing optimum adaptation 
to the catenary. The spring action on the sliding strips works by means of a cylindrical com-
pression spring which makes it possible for the sliding strips to react quickly to irregularities 
in the catenary. 
Deflector plates which have been tested in the wind tunnel are used to achieve a balanced 
dynamic component where contact pressure with the catenary is concerned, when the panto-
graph is at different lifting heights and with different directions of travel. 
The four end-horns are made of plastic reinforced with fibre glass. 
Pneumatic drive 
The low installation height required for the pantograph led to the adaption of a new driving 
system in which the tension springs are stretched by an air cylinder to raise the pantograph, 
and released by it to lower the pantograph. Because of this it was possible for the air cylinder 
to be very short in size, and to be simply constructed. Neither restoring springs nor gas com-
